coredata.dc:title: Biomineralization of calcium carbonate under amino acid carbon dots and its application in bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Zongqi
Yang, Tingyu
Liang, Tiantian
Wu, Zhouying
Wu, Ting
Zhang, Jianbin
Yu, Lan
coredata.dc:description: Biomineralization is a common phenomenon in nature, and organisms can prepare functional materials with multi-level ordered structures through biomineralization. Herein, inspired by biomineralization, for the first time, we developed an amino acid quantum dots (ACDs)–CO2-Ca(OH)2 system to study the biomineralization process of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs. The ACDs could not only control the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 but also could be doped into CaCO3 during the formation of CaCO3 to form ACDs@CaCO3 organic–inorganic hybrid fluorescent materials which had excellent luminous ability. Meanwhile, the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 could also be controlled by controlling the CO2 flow rate and rotation speed. After that, a possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs was proposed. Finally, the synthetic spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were applied to the field of biological imaging for the first time. Our work not only provides new bionic methods for the synthesis of multifunctional biomineralized materials and new insights for further understanding of biomineralization mechanism, but also the synthesized new biomineralization fluorescent materials have great application potential in the field of bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomineralization
Calcium carbonate
Amino acid carbon dots
Morphology and crystal form
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of ACDs concentration on the property of CaCO3
Influence of CO2 flow rate on the properties of CaCO3
Influence of rotation speed on the properties of CaCO3
Possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 under ACDs
In vitro cytotoxicity test of ACDs@CaCO3
In vitro cell imaging of spherical ACDs@CaCO3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Scheme of microwave assisted one-step synthesis of highly photoluminescent ACDs. (B) TEM image of ACDs. (C) HR-TEM image of ACDs. (D) Size distribution graph of ACDs.

(A) FT-IR spectrum of ACDs. (B) XRD of ACDs. (C) XPS of ACDs. (D) UV–Vis absorption of ACDs. (E) PL emission spectra of ACDs (λex = 360 nm). (F) Photographs of the dispersions of ACDs under daylight and a UV lamp at 365 nm.

SEM images of CaCO3 crystals at 1200 rpm rotation speed with 40 mL/min CO2 flow rate and different ACDs concentrations: A, 0 mg/mL; B, 0.2 mg/mL; C, 0.4 mg/mL; D, 0.6 mg/mL (rod-like (yellow), dumbbell-like (red) and inflorescence-like (green)); E, 0.8 mg/mL and F, 1.0 mg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

FTIR spectra (I) and XRD patterns (II) of CaCO3 crystals at 1200 rpm rotation speed with 40 mL/min CO2 flow rate and different ACDs concentrations: A, 0 mg/mL; B, 0.2 mg/mL; C, 0.4 mg/mL; D, 0.6 mg/mL; E, 0.8 mg/mL and F, 1.0 mg/mL.

CLSM images of CaCO3 samples were obtained at different ACDs concentrations under CLSM. (Blue: λex = 405 nm, scale bar = 50 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SEM of CaCO3 crystals at different CO2 flow rates at 1200 rpm rotation speed: A, 40 mL/min; B, 60 mL/min (rod-like (yellow), dumbbell-like (red) and inflorescence-like (green)); C, 80 mL/min; D, 100 mL/min; E, 120 mL/min and F, 140 mL/min with 1.0 mg/mL ACDs concentration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM of CaCO3 crystals at different rotation speeds at 40 mL/min CO2 flow rate: A, 1200 rpm; B, 1250 rpm; C, 1300 rpm; D, 1350 rpm; E, 1400 rpm and F, 1450 rpm with 1.0 mg/mL ACDs concentration.
Possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 under ACDs.

CLSM images of CaCO3 intermediate (rod-like, dumbbell-like and inflorescence-like) obtained at an ACDs concentration of 0.6 mg/mL. (Blue: λex = 405 nm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Cell viability of MGC-803 cells after incubating with (A) ACDs and (B) spherical ACDs@CaCO3.

CLSM images of MGC-803 cells after incubating with spherical ACDs@CaCO3 at 24 h and 48 h. (Blue: λex = 405 nm, scale bar = 100 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 crystals at 1200 rpm rotation speed with 40 mL/min CO2 flow rate and different ACDs concentrations: A, 0 mg/mL; B, 0.2 mg/mL; C, 0.4 mg/mL; D, 0.6 mg/mL; E, 0.8 mg/mL and F, 1.0 mg/mL.

head.ce:title: Biomineralization of calcium carbonate under amino acid carbon dots and its application in bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inner Mongolia People’s Hospital, Hohhot 010010, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inner Mongolia People’s Hospital, Hohhot 010010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot 010051, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University of Technology, Hohhot 010051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving
School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Provincial Key Lab of Green Chemical Technology and High Efficient Energy Saving, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomineralization is a common phenomenon in nature, and organisms can prepare functional materials with multi-level ordered structures through biomineralization. Herein, inspired by biomineralization, for the first time, we developed an amino acid quantum dots (ACDs)–CO2-Ca(OH)2 system to study the biomineralization process of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs. The ACDs could not only control the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 but also could be doped into CaCO3 during the formation of CaCO3 to form ACDs@CaCO3 organic–inorganic hybrid fluorescent materials which had excellent luminous ability. Meanwhile, the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 could also be controlled by controlling the CO2 flow rate and rotation speed. After that, a possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs was proposed. Finally, the synthetic spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were applied to the field of biological imaging for the first time. Our work not only provides new bionic methods for the synthesis of multifunctional biomineralized materials and new insights for further understanding of biomineralization mechanism, but also the synthesized new biomineralization fluorescent materials have great application potential in the field of bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomineralization is a widespread phenomenon in nature, which realizes the combination of organic organisms and inorganic materials [1]. Inspired by biomineralization, researchers have used the principle of biomineralization to prepare organic–inorganic composite structural materials similar to biominerals in vitro, such as bionic teeth, bionic bones, and bionic shells [2]. These materials show good performance in dental restoration, bone repair, and hard tissue replacement [1,2]. The regulation of the formation process of inorganic minerals by organic biomolecules in biomineralization research, as well as the relationship between the function and chemical structure of biominerals, has brought inspiration to the research and development of functional materials [1,2]. In particular, studies on the mechanism of biomineralization can provide theoretical guidance for the design and preparation of next-generation functional materials.

Calcium carbonate (CaCO3) is one of the most abundant minerals in various biominerals. It has three anhydrous crystalline polymorphic forms (calcite, aragonite, and vaterite), and the amorphous calcium carbonate (ACC) [3]. To control the polymorphs and morphology of CaCO3 biomineralization process, researchers begin to introduce organics as a template in the CaCO3 mineralization process to simulate the natural biomineralization process [4,5]. These organics include amino acids, peptides, proteins, polysaccharides, etc [6,7]. The synthesis of CaCO3 with different morphology and crystal form can apply to various fields [8–11]. For example, spherical CaCO3 can apply to biomedicine, and rhombic calcite CaCO3 can be used for rubber filling.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive attention as one of the most important components of carbon-based photoluminescence (PL) nanomaterials in recent years [12]. Due to their extraordinary optoelectronic properties, such as high photostability, easy synthesis and functionalization, good biocompatibility and adjustable composition, CDs are widely used in various fields, including nanoprobes, optoelectronic devices, catalysis and biomedicine [13,14]. In addition, owing to the rich surface functional groups, it has been used to construct CDs@CaCO3 fluorescent hybrids [15–17]. This indicates that CDs can also be used as an organic template to participate in the biomineralization process of CaCO3. However, what these studies concentrate on is only one of these aspets of CDs@CaCO3: structrue and morphology [15–17], or fluorescence intensity and stability [16], failing to elucidate the changes of CaCO3 in the morphology and crystal form when CDs presents. Meanwhile, there is a lack of comprehensive and detailed research process and mechanism research on the construction of CDs@CaCO3 hybrid luminescent functional materials. Therefore, a detailed study of the mechanism of biomineralization holds great promise for the design and application of CDs@CaCO3. Most importantly, although these studies have synthesized this kind of CDs@CaCO3, there are few follow-up developments and applications.

Herein, inspired by the biomineralization of natural CaCO3, for the first time, we developed an ACDs-CO2-Ca(OH)2 system to realize the biomineralization process of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs (Fig. S1). First, the ACDs were prepared through a one-step microwave pyrolysis method, where the L-Cysteine (L-Cys) present in the organism was used as nitrogen source, while citric acid was carbon source. Next, the biomineralization process of CaCO3 under different ACDs concentrations, CO2 flow rates, and rotating speeds was studied. In this process, the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 could be controlled. At low ACDs concentration, calcite spherical CaCO3 particles formed, and at high ACDs concentration, ACC aggregates were obtained. Due to the existence of ACDs, the prepared CaCO3 samples also had apparent blue fluorescence, forming a kind of ACDs@CaCO3 organic–inorganic hybrid fluorescent material. In addition, a possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs was proposed. Finally, the synthetic spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were applied to the field of biological imaging for the first time. The results showed that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 have very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be used as a fluorescent probe to be applied to the field of cell imaging.

The L-Cys (Art.No. C108237, 99 %) and quinine sulfate dihydrate (Art.No. Q110176, 99 %, Fluorescence analysis) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China). The Citric acid (Art.No. 30196768, 99 %, Anhydrous) and Ca(OH)2 (Art.No. 20011618, 95 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 5(6)-CFDA, SE (Art.No. C4070, 90 %) was purchased from Shanghai Baisai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water (DI water, 18.2 MΩ·cm) was used in all experiments. Human MGC-803 gastric cancer cell lines (MGC-803) were purchased from the National Infrastructure of Cell Line Resource (STR profiling).

The transmission electron micrograph (HR-TEM) of ACDs was obtained on an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 high-resolution transmission electron microscope with an operating voltage of 200 kV. The hydrodynamic size measurement (Malvern NanoZS 90, Malvern, UK) of ACDs was characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS). XPS spectra were obtained with an ESCALAB 250Xi XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The scanning electron microscopy images (SEM) of CaCO3 samples were obtained by Quanta FEG 650, USA scanning electron microscope (operating voltage 20 kV). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained on an iS50 Nicolet FT-IR spectrometer, and the measurement range is from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at room temperature. UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-3200 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-7000 FL Spectrophotometer (150 W xenon lamp). Decay time was calculated by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS980). The surface area and pore size (3H-2000PS2 BET instrument) were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda model, respectively. The crystal form of CaCO3 was characterized by an X-ray powder diffractometer (Siemens D/max-RB), and taken Cu-Kα (λ = 1.541874 Å). Cell imaging was used Leica SP8X Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (CLSM).

Herein, the ACDs were prepared through a one-step microwave pyrolysis method, where the L-Cys were used as nitrogen source, while citric acid as carbon source (Fig. 1 A). Specifically, the L-Cys and citric acid started to react and decomposed gradually, followed by the carbonization to form ACDs when the microwave power was 700 W. As shown in Fig. 1B, the TEM image revealed that the ACDs were spherical in morphology and well dispersed. The size distribution of the ACDs was relatively small and the average diameter was about 3.25 nm (Fig. 1D), which was well consistent with the size detected by DLS (Fig. S2), where the average of ACDs was about 3.62 nm. The HRTEM image of ACDs (Fig. 1C) indicated that the ACDs possess distorted lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of about 0.186 nm. This disordered carbon phase was further supported by the XRD pattern of ACDs, which displayed a broad and weak peak centered at 20.36° (Fig. 2 B) [18].

The surface composition of ACDs was characterized by XPS and FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2A, the broad bands centered at 3430, 3220, and 2930 cm−1 correspond to O-H, –NH2 and –CH2 groups, respectively. The peak at 2570 cm−1 was attributed to the S-H group. The peaks at 1720, 1620, and 1500 cm−1 were ascribed to C = O, N-H and C-N stretching vibration, respectively. The peak around 1210 cm−1 was attributed to C-O [19–21]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) of the ACDs exhibited four peaks at 532.1, 400.1, 285.0, 228.0 and 164.1 eV, which were attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, S2s and S2p, respectively [22–24]. The results indicated that the ACDs contained C, N, O and S in a weight ratio of 69.30: 5.51: 20.66: 4.53. The FTIR and XPS spectra indicated that abundant SH, CONH2 and COOH groups were present on the surface of ACDs. Therefore, ACDs mainly consist of a carbogenic core with SH, CONH2 and COOH as the surface passivating agent.

To explore the optical properties of the ACDs, UV–vis absorption and PL studies were carried out. The prepared ACDs in solution showed a strong absorption peak centered at 349 nm (Fig. 2D), similar to the typical π-π* transition of C = C bonds [25]. As shown in Fig. 2E, the PL emission spectrum ACDs in aqueous solution exhibited a peak centered at 447 nm when excited by 360 nm light. The photograph in Fig. 2F showed the optical image of the aqueous dispersion of ACDs irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp. The quantum yield of the photoluminescent ACDs was estimated to be 53.4 % by calibrating against quinine sulfate (excited at 360 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of ACDs concentration on the property of CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by biomineralization, for the first time, we developed an ACDs-CO2-Ca(OH)2 system to study the biomineralization process of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the morphology of CaCO3 was significantly influenced by the ACDs concentration. The CaCO3 crystals obtained in the absence of ACDs were smooth single rhombohedral particles (Fig. 3A). The crystals were identified by FTIR and XRD as pure calcite (Fig. 4 I A and 4II B). The characteristic peaks of calcite at 875 cm−1, 711 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1 were due to the out-of-plane bending (υ2 mode), in-plane bending (υ4 mode) and asymmetric stretching (υ3 mode) of CO3 2– (Fig. 4I A) [26–30]. The distinctive diffraction peaks at 2θ = 22.92°, 29.32°, 35.90°, 43.04°, 47.42° and 48.42° of CaCO3 crystals were corresponding to the (012), (104), (110), (202), (018) and (116) lattice planes of calcite, respectively [31,32]. When ACDs were added to the reaction, the morphology of CaCO3 had undergone a fundamental change (Fig. 3B ∼ F). The morphology of CaCO3 changed from diamond-shaped block to monodisperse spherical particles (Fig. 3B) when the concentration of ACDs was 0.2 mg/mL. As the concentration of ACDs increased, the morphology of CaCO3 became irregular aggregates with many spherical structures gathered together (Fig. 3C, D). At the same time, when the ACDs concentration was 0.6 mg/mL, very small CaCO3 particles were formed (Fig. 3D). In addition to the spherical particles, these small particles also appeared rod-like, dumbbell-like and inflorescence-like (Fig. S3). Within this concentration range (0.2 ∼ 0.6 mg/mL), the crystal form of CaCO3 was similar to the previous crystal form of calcite (Fig. 4B ∼ D).

The CaCO3 samples with different ACDs concentrations were observed and photographed under CLSM, and images of different conditions (Bright field, Blue, Merge) were obtained as shown in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5, when no ACDs were added, blue fluorescence was not observed in the obtained CaCO3. When the ACDs concentration was 0.2 mg/mL, CaCO3 had obvious blue fluorescence emission. As shown in Fig. S5, the CaCO3 obtained at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL was spherical particles, which was consistent with the morphology of CaCO3 observed under SEM. At the same time, the single-particle CaCO3 obtained had apparent blue fluorescence emission. It could be seen from Supplemental Video 1 that the inside of CaCO3 particles obtained also has obvious blue fluorescence. This indicated that ACDs were involved in the entire formation process of CaCO3 and could be doped into CaCO3 instead of simple surface adsorption to cause CaCO3 to emit light. With the increase of ACDs concentration (0.4 ∼ 1.0 mg/mL), the obtained CaCO3 samples basically had obvious blue fluorescence and were consistent with the morphology (Fig. 3) of CaCO3 observed under SEM. When the ACDs concentration was 1.0 mg/mL, the obtained ACC aggregates had stronger blue fluorescence compared to the CaCO3 obtained at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL ACDs (Fig. S5). It could also be clearly observed from Supplemental Video 2 that the inside of CaCO3 aggregates also had stronger blue fluorescence. Therefore, the ACDs could not only control the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 but also could be doped into CaCO3 during the formation of CaCO3 to form ACDs@CaCO3 organic–inorganic hybrid materials, so that the synthesized ACDs@CaCO3 had fluorescence characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CO2 flow rate on the properties of CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: The flow of CO2 affected the content of CO3 2– in the solution. The content of CO3 2– was directly related to the degree of supersaturation and had a significant impact on the crystallization process of CaCO3 [39–41]. As shown in Fig. 6 , CO2 flow rate had a great influence on the morphology of CaCO3. The flow rate was 40 mL/min, CaCO3 was ACC (Fig. 6A), which was an aggregate composed of many nano-sized particles. As the flow rate increased, the morphology of CaCO3 samples changed significantly, and the morphology of CaCO3 changed from aggregates to dispersed spherical CaCO3 particles (Fig. 6B ∼ F). However, some other morphological CaCO3 structures such as dumbbell-like (Fig. 6B ∼ C) and inflorescence-like (Fig. 6E ∼ F) could also be found in these CaCO3 samples. At the same time, when the flow rate of CO2 was 60 mL/min, it could be clearly seen from the cross-section of the broken CaCO3 particles in Fig. S6 that the spherical CaCO3 particles were self-assembled by nano-scale fine rod-shaped CaCO3. FTIR spectra and XRD patterns of CaCO3 were obtained and the results are shown in Fig. S7. When the flow rate was changed, the crystal form of CaCO3 changed significantly. From ACC (Fig. S7 A) to calcite (Fig. S7 B). FTIR spectra of CaCO3 had a characteristic peak at 866 cm−1 indicating that ACC formed. FTIR spectra had two characteristic peaks at 875 and 711 cm−1 indicating calcite formed. It could be seen from the XRD patterns (Fig. S7II A ∼ B) that the CaCO3 sample changed from no pronounced diffraction peaks to obvious calcite diffraction peaks. But when the crystal form of CaCO3 was changed to calcite, continuing to increase flow rate will not change the crystal form of CaCO3, which was pure calcite (Fig. S7 C ∼ F). Therefore, the morphology and crystal form (Table S1) of CaCO3 could be controlled and ACC was converted into calcite spherical CaCO3 particles by controlling the flow rate of CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of rotation speed on the properties of CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: The change of rotation speed will affect the mutual contact of gas and liquid, resulting in a change in the content of CO3 2– in the solution, thereby affecting the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 [42,43]. As shown in Fig. 7 , when the rotation speed increase was small (1200 ∼ 1300 rpm), the morphology of CaCO3 was basically irregular aggregates (Fig. 7A ∼ C). FTIR spectra and XRD patterns (Fig. S8 A ∼ C) clearly proved that CaCO3 sample was ACC. FTIR spectra of CaCO3 had a characteristic peak at 866 cm−1 indicating that ACC formed. The XRD patterns showed no diffraction peak, which indicated that the sample was not crystalline. With the further increase of the rotation speed (1350 ∼ 1400 rpm), the morphology of CaCO3 changed significantly, irregular aggregates began to disappear, and spherical CaCO3 particles appeared (Fig. 7D ∼ E). When the rotation speed was 1400 rpm, the irregular aggregates disappeared completely, and the obtained CaCO3 samples were all spherical CaCO3 particles. And there were a large number of small-sized CaCO3 particles that were generated with a size of about 5 ∼ 10 μm. According to FTIR spectra and XRD patterns (Fig. S8 D ∼ F), it was proved that the crystal form of the spherical CaCO3 particles obtained was pure calcite. Therefore, increasing the rotation speed of the reaction can realize the control of the morphology and crystal form (Table S2) of CaCO3, and transform the ACC into calcite spherical CaCO3 particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 under ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The process of biomineralization in nature is very complicated. Researchers are studying the biomineralization process of CaCO3 by organic substances such as proteins and amino acids and found that these substances mainly affect the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 through the interaction of COOH and other groups with Ca2+ [44–47]. Therefore, the SH, CONH2 and COOH groups on the surface of ACDs played a significant role in the nucleation and growth of CaCO3.

As shown in Fig. S9, the zeta potential of the aqueous solution of ACDs was − 28.36 mV. When the aqueous solution of ACDs was added to the Ca(OH)2 solution, the zeta potential of the solution increased to + 8.24 mV. This indicated that the ACDs had an electrostatic interaction with the Ca2+ in the solution, and the Ca2+ were adsorbed on the surface of the ACDs. Due to the presence of functional groups such as COOH on the surface of ACDs, ACDs exhibited obvious negative charge in solution. Therefore, when ACDs were added to Ca(OH)2 solution, the negative electricity on the surface of ACDs will interact electrostatically with Ca2+, and Ca2+ was adsorbed to surface to form an ACDs-Ca complex.

The possible biomineralization mechanism of CaCO3 is shown in Fig. 8 . The CO2 gas was introduced into the solution, CO3 2– gradually formed. With the increase of CO3 2–, the electrostatic interaction will combine with Ca2+. In this process, it will gradually reach a supersaturated state of CaCO3 and begin to form CaCO3 nuclei. As the saturation further increases, a large number of CaCO3 nuclei form and rapidly grow into ACC (Fig. 8A). ACC was a particularly unstable state in the solution, and it was easily transformed into CaCO3 of other crystal types and morphology [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 8B, with the prolongation of the reaction time, ACC will undergo two transformations. One was to direct aggregate to form ACC aggregates. The other was dissolution-reprecipitation process and transformed into calcite needle-like subunits. With the formation of a large number of needle-like subunits, these needle-like subunits undergo a self-assembly process, undergoing a rod-like, dumbbell-like and inflorescence-like process to form calcite CaCO3 spherical particles.

Direct aggregation and dissolution-re-precipitation were two competing approaches. The core reason for these two competitive pathways was the electrostatic interaction between CO3 2– and ACDs for Ca2+, which was the existence of a competitive relationship between CO3 2– and ACDs. When the concentration of ACDs was higher (0.8 ∼ 1.0 mg/mL), there was a lot of ACDs in the solution, a lot of ACDs-Ca complexes will be formed, which will inhibit the supersaturation of CaCO3, reduce the nucleation rate of CaCO3, and reduce the rate of conversion to ACC. The process leading to the formation of ACC was slow. At the same time, ACDs-Ca lost Ca2+ and turned into ACDs. In this way, a large number of free ACDs will appear in the solution. Due to the presence of groups such as –COOH on the surface of ACDs, they will interact with Ca2+ or CO3 2– on the surface of ACC to inhibit the transformation of ACC into other crystal forms of CaCO3 and thus inhibit the dissolution-re-precipitation process. ACC could only undergo an aggregation process to form aggregates under the action of ACDs. When the concentration of ACDs was low (0.2 ∼ 0.6 mg/mL), the concentration of ACDs in the solution was low, which was not enough to inhibit the supersaturation of CaCO3, leading to nucleation and the formation of ACC faster. Moreover, due to the presence of fewer ACDs in the solution, the weak interaction with ACC was not sufficient to maintain the stability of ACC, and ACC quickly entered the dissolution-re-precipitation process to form calcite CaCO3 particles. Although the content of ACDs was small and insufficient to stabilize ACC, the presence of functional groups such as surface COOH will also interact with Ca2+ or CO3 2– on the surface of CaCO3. The process leading to the formation of spherical CaCO3 particles was a self-assembly process from needle-like subunits to rod-like, dumbbell-like, inflorescence-like and spherical. As shown in Fig. 9 , CLSM images of a CaCO3 sample obtained at an ACDs concentration of 0.6 mg/mL, showed a clear intermediate CaCO3 morphology (rod-like, dumbbell-like and inflorescence-like). And these CaCO3 intermediates could also clearly observe blue fluorescence under CLSM, which indicated that ACDs participated in and affected the entire self-assembly process of the formation of spherical CaCO3.

Therefore, the competition mechanism between ACDs and CO3 2– and the interaction between ACDs surface functional groups and CaCO3 surface resulted in the formation of two different CaCO3 processes (Direct aggregation and Dissolution-re-precipitation). Under certain conditions of CO2 flow rate and rotation speed, high ACDs concentration will cause CaCO3 to carry out the direct aggregation pathway. Low ACDs concentration will allow CaCO3 to carry out the dissolution-re-precipitation pathway. The process of transforming high-concentration ACDs to low-concentration ACDs was also a process of transforming ACC to calcite spherical CaCO3. This was a reasonable explanation for the formation of many CaCO3 intermediates (rod-like, dumbbell-like and inflorescence-like) in the CaCO3 samples obtained at the intermediate ACDs concentration (0.6 mg/mL). During our experiment, the control of CO3 2– was mainly controlled by flow rate and rotation speed. According to the competition mechanism, under certain conditions of ACDs concentration, high CO3 2– concentration will cause CaCO3 to undergo a process of dissolution-re-precipitation. On the contrary, it will undergo a process of direct aggregation. When the flow rate or rotation speed was increased, a large amount of CO2 will be converted into CO3 2–. The increase of CO3 2– will increase the saturation of CaCO3 more quickly, leading to the formation of a large number of CaCO3 nuclei, and the rapid conversion into ACC. In this process, ACDs could not stabilize ACC very well, so the formation way of CaCO3 was the dissolution-re-precipitation way. This reasonably explained the regulation of the morphology (Figs. 6 and 7) and crystal form (Fig. S7 and S8) of CaCO3 by flow rate and rotation speed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity test of ACDs@CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: The CaCO3 with different morphology and crystal form can be synthesized by controlling different reaction conditions. Considering the size of biological cells and the way of ingesting materials [50,51], we selected spherical ACDs@CaCO3 synthesized under the conditions of an ACDs concentration of 1 mg/mL, a flow rate of 60 mL/min, and a rotation speed of 1200 rpm for subsequent research.

To evaluate the biological safety of ACDs and spherical ACDs@CaCO3, the vitro cytotoxic effects of ACDs and spherical ACDs@CaCO3 on MGC-803 cancer cells were evaluated by MTT colorimetric assay and the results were shown in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10A, it could be seen that the cell viability of MGC-803 decreased slightly after 48 h of incubating with lower concentrations (0 ∼ 400 μg/mL) of the ACDs and the cell viability was above 97.91 %. With the increase of ACDs concentration, the cell viability decreased rapidly, even at the concentration of 1000 μg/mL, the cell viability was still more than 77.95 %. This indicated that ACDs had lower cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 10B, MGC-803 cells were incubated for 48 h with different spherical ACDs@CaCO3 concentrations. The cell viability was maintained at about 98.49%, indicating that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were basically non-cytotoxic. However, at 1000 μg/mL concentrations, spherical ACDs@CaCO3 (98.49 %) exhibited lower toxicity to cells compared to ACDs (77.95 %). The reason may be that CaCO3 itself has better biocompatibility and biodegradability. These results revealed that ACDs and spherical ACDs@CaCO3 have good biocompatibility, lower cell cytotoxicity and could be used in various applications for in vitro as well as in vivo studies. In particular, the low toxicity of spherical ACDs@CaCO3 indicated that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 synthesized through the regulation of ACDs have a good biological application prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell imaging of spherical ACDs@CaCO3
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have been widely used in the field of bioimaging due to their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [52,53]. The cellular imaging studies of ACDs were in Supplemental Information S2. Based on this, the synthesized spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were applied to the field of biological imaging for the first time. The fluorescence properties of spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were investigated in Supplemental Information S3.

As shown in Fig. 11 , the imaging condition of spherical ACDs@CaCO3 in MGC-803 was observed through CLSM after spherical ACDs@CaCO3 and MGC-803 were incubated for different times. After 24 h of co-cultivation, it is obvious that MGC-803 were in a better state, and a large number of spherical ACDs@CaCO3 can also be seen in the bright field. This again showed that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 have lower cytotoxicity to MGC-803. From the blue fluorescence and merged images, it can be clearly observed that a single spherical ACDs@CaCO3 particle also emitted blue fluorescence. Meanwhile, MGC-803 can also emit blue fluorescence. This indicated that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 can be well swallowed by MGC-803, and are decomposed to release ACDs in MGC-803, causing MGC-803 to emit blue fluorescence. From Figs. S10 and S11 and Supplemental Video 3, it could be clearly seen that the spherical ACDs@CaCO3 are phagocytosed by the MGC-803 and located inside the MGC-803. Especially from Supplemental Video 3, it could be observed that a part of spherical ACDs@CaCO3 is located in the lower part of the plane and emits a clear blue fluorescence, which fully proves that spherical ACDs@CaCO3 are located in the interior of MGC-803. After 48 h of incubation, spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were seen to be significantly reduced, and the blue fluorescence of MGC-803 was further enhanced. This showed that more spherical ACDs@CaCO3 are swallowed by MGC-803 over time, causing MGC-803 to emit stronger blue fluorescence. Likewise, under equivalent imaging conditions, no blue fluorescence was detected in the blank control cells incubated without spherical ACDs@CaCO3 (Fig. S12), indicating no spontaneous background fluorescence under this imaging condition. Therefore, spherical ACDs@CaCO3 synthesized by ACDs biomineralization could be well applied to biological cell imaging.

In summary, inspired by biomineralization, for the first time, we developed an ACDs-CO2-Ca(OH)2 system to study the biomineralization process of CaCO3 in the presence of ACDs. The ACDs could not only control the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 but also were doped into CaCO3 during the formation of CaCO3 to form ACDs@CaCO3 organic–inorganic hybrid fluorescent materials which have excellent luminous ability. At low ACDs concentration (0.2 ∼ 0.6 mg/mL), calcite spherical CaCO3 particles formed, and at high ACDs concentration (0.8 ∼ 1.0 mg/mL), ACC aggregates were obtained. Meanwhile, in the presence of ACDs, the morphology and crystal form of CaCO3 could be controlled by controlling the CO2 flow rate and rotation speed. At high flow rate (60 ∼ 140 mg/mL) and rotation speed (1350 ∼ 1450 rpm), calcite spherical CaCO3 particles formed, and at low flow rate (40 mg/mL) and rotation speed (1200 ∼ 1300 rpm), ACC aggregates were obtained. Finally, spherical ACDs@CaCO3 were applied to MGC-803 cell imaging for the first time. The spherical ACDs@CaCO3 had very low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and showed good imaging capabilities for MGC-803 cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81960449).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2022.110644.

ce:section.ce:para:
